class_schedule.topic: The Publishing Ecosystem: From Manuscript to Marketplace
learning_objectives: Map the contemporary publishing ecosystem: traditional, independent, academic, and self-publishing
Identify the roles in the publishing process: agent, editor, designer, marketer, distributor
Understand how publishing economics shape what gets published and how
readings: Publishers Weekly, selected recent industry articles

lecture_notes.introduction: Publishing is the bridge between writing and reading. Understanding how the publishing industry works — its economics, its gatekeeping, its transformation by digital technology — is essential for anyone who wants to participate in literary culture as a writer, editor, or publisher.
key_concepts: The Big Five publishers and the concentration of the industry
The agent's role: gatekeeping, advocacy, and market knowledge
Editorial roles: acquisitions editor, developmental editor, copyeditor, proofreader
The economics of publishing: advances, royalties, print runs, and the blockbuster model
Independent and small press publishing: alternatives to the corporate model
lecture_notes.demonstration: Tracing the journey of a real book from manuscript submission through publication, identifying every decision point and the person responsible at each stage

review.value: Contemporary publishing increasingly depends on a small number of bestsellers to subsidize the rest of the list — a model that incentivizes safe, marketable books over experimental or niche ones

A manuscript typically passes through an agent (who selects it from hundreds of submissions), an acquisitions editor (who champions it internally), and an editorial board (who approves the financial investment) before a publisher commits to publication

learning_objectives: Apply developmental editing principles to a book-length manuscript
Write an effective editorial letter that addresses structural issues with specificity and tact
Distinguish between the editor's role (identifying problems) and the author's role (solving them)
readings: Saller, The Subversive Copy Editor, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Developmental editing is the most intellectually demanding work in publishing. The developmental editor reads a manuscript not as a reader but as a diagnostician, identifying structural, narrative, and conceptual problems and articulating them in a way that empowers the author to solve them.
key_concepts: The editorial letter: a comprehensive, tactful assessment of a manuscript's strengths and needed revisions
Structural editing: pacing, organization, chapter order, and overall architecture
Content editing: argument development, evidence, character arc, thematic coherence
The editor-author relationship: collaboration based on trust, honesty, and respect for the author's vision
When to intervene and when to trust the writer: the editor's judgment call
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reading a sample editorial letter together, analyzing how the editor identifies specific problems, provides concrete examples, suggests possible solutions without prescribing them, and maintains a supportive tone

review.value: A written assessment (typically 5-15 pages for a book manuscript) that identifies the manuscript's strengths, its structural and content problems, and suggested directions for revision — the most important document in the editor-author relationship
The editor identifies problems and explains why they are problems; the author decides how to solve them. Good editing empowers the writer; bad editing takes over the manuscript.

class_schedule.topic: Copyediting and Proofreading: The Final Polish
learning_objectives: Apply copyediting techniques to ensure consistency, accuracy, and clarity
Use standard copyediting marks and queries
Understand the difference between copyediting (improving prose) and proofreading (catching errors in final proof)
readings: Chicago Manual of Style, Ch. 2 (Manuscript Preparation) and selected sections

lecture_notes.introduction: Copyediting is invisible when done well and glaring when done badly. The copyeditor ensures that every sentence is grammatically correct, every fact is verified, every spelling is consistent, and every style guideline is followed — without altering the author's voice.
key_concepts: Copyediting scope: grammar, punctuation, spelling, consistency, fact-checking, style guide adherence
Standard copyediting marks: insertion, deletion, transposition, capitalization, and query marks
The style sheet: a copyeditor's record of editorial decisions for consistency
Author queries: how to flag problems and ask questions without overstepping
Proofreading vs. copyediting: proofreading checks the typeset proof against the edited manuscript, catching errors introduced in production
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on copyediting workshop: students copyedit a manuscript page using standard marks, then compare their decisions with a professional copyeditor's work on the same page

review.value: A document the copyeditor creates for each project, recording decisions about spelling, hyphenation, capitalization, and other style choices to ensure consistency throughout — especially important for names, technical terms, and series style

When a copyeditor encounters an ambiguity, factual question, or potential error, they write a query rather than making a unilateral change — queries are phrased as questions or suggestions, respecting the author's authority over their own text

learning_objectives: Understand the principles of book design: typography, layout, and cover design
Analyze how marketing and publicity shape a book's reception
Identify the role of metadata, reviews, and social media in contemporary book marketing
readings: Sample book marketing plans (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: A book is a designed object. Its typography, layout, cover, and physical form communicate before a single word is read. And in a marketplace flooded with titles, marketing determines whether a book reaches its audience at all.
key_concepts: Typography: font selection, leading, margins, and their effects on readability and tone
Cover design: the cover as the book's primary marketing tool — genre signals, visual identity, and shelf impact
Interior layout: chapter openings, running heads, pagination, and the reading experience
Marketing fundamentals: positioning, audience identification, comp titles, and launch strategy
Digital marketing: metadata, SEO, social media, and the importance of discoverability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing three covers for the same novel (different editions or international versions), discussing how each cover positions the book for a different audience and market

review.value: Font choice communicates before the reader processes the words: a serif font suggests tradition and seriousness; a sans-serif font suggests modernity and accessibility; a handwritten font suggests intimacy or informality — every typographic decision is a reading instruction

In a digital marketplace, a book that cannot be found cannot be sold. Metadata (keywords, categories, descriptions) determines whether a book appears in search results — making it as important as the book's content for reaching readers

class_schedule.topic: The Future of Publishing: Digital, Independent, and Global
learning_objectives: Analyze how digital technology has transformed publishing economics, distribution, and reader access
Evaluate the opportunities and challenges of self-publishing and independent publishing
Understand the global publishing landscape and the politics of literary translation and distribution
readings: Phillips, Turning the Page: The Evolution of the Book, Ch. 8-9
Selected articles on the current state of publishing (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Publishing is in the middle of the most significant transformation since Gutenberg. Digital distribution, self-publishing, audiobooks, and global markets are reshaping who can publish, how, and for whom. The question is not whether publishing will survive but what it will become.
key_concepts: E-books and audiobooks: how digital formats change reading behavior and business models
Self-publishing: the democratization of access and the challenge of quality and discoverability
Independent publishers: the role of small presses in literary culture and diversity
Global publishing: translation economics, English-language dominance, and the politics of literary circulation
The future editor: how editorial roles are evolving in a digital, global, and algorithmically mediated marketplace
lecture_notes.demonstration: Panel discussion format: students take assigned roles (traditional publisher, self-published author, literary agent, independent bookstore owner) and debate the future of the industry

review.value: Digital platforms have eliminated the gatekeeping function of traditional publishing, allowing anyone to publish — but have transferred the challenges of quality control, editing, design, and marketing from the publisher to the individual author

Only about 3% of books published in the US are translations, compared to 25-50% in many European countries — this statistic reveals the English-language publishing world's insularity and its implications for which literatures circulate globally

quizzes.topic: The Publishing Industry and Editorial Roles

questions.question: What is the difference between developmental editing and copyediting? At what stage of the publishing process does each occur?
questions.answer: Developmental editing addresses structural and content issues — pacing, argument, character development, organization — and occurs early, when the manuscript is still being revised. Copyediting addresses sentence-level issues — grammar, consistency, style, and accuracy — and occurs after the developmental revision is complete and the manuscript's structure is final. Developmental editing shapes what the book says; copyediting ensures it says it correctly.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes the scope of each type
Accurately places each in the production timeline
Explains the relationship between the two

questions.question: What is the 'blockbuster model' in publishing, and how does it affect what gets published?
questions.answer: The blockbuster model means that publishers increasingly depend on a small number of bestselling titles to generate the revenue that subsidizes the rest of their list. This incentivizes acquiring books with proven mass-market appeal — celebrity memoirs, sequels, franchise tie-ins — and makes it harder for experimental, niche, or debut authors to receive significant investment. The model concentrates resources on potential blockbusters at the expense of diverse, risky, or literary titles.

rubric.key: Explains the effect on what gets published
Notes the impact on diverse or experimental work

questions.question: Why is metadata important in contemporary book publishing? Give two specific examples of metadata that affect a book's discoverability.
questions.answer: Metadata — keywords, categories, descriptions, and subject codes — determines whether a book appears in search results on retailer websites and library databases. Two examples: (1) BISAC subject codes categorize the book for placement in online and physical stores; an incorrect code means the book appears in the wrong category. (2) Keywords in the book's online listing determine search engine visibility; a book about the Harlem Renaissance needs keywords like 'African American literature,' 'Jazz Age,' and 'Langston Hughes' to appear in relevant searches.

rubric.key: Explains why metadata matters for discoverability

questions.question: What is a 'style sheet' in copyediting, and why is it essential for maintaining consistency?
questions.answer: A style sheet is a document the copyeditor creates for each project, recording decisions about spelling variants (gray/grey), hyphenation (e-mail/email), capitalization, number formatting, character name spellings, and other choices where the style guide does not prescribe a single correct form. It is essential because a 300-page manuscript involves thousands of such decisions, and human memory alone cannot maintain consistency. The style sheet ensures that page 250 follows the same conventions as page 5.

rubric.key: Accurate definition of the style sheet
Provides specific examples of recorded decisions
Explains why it is essential for consistency

major_assignments.title: Editorial Project
major_assignments.description: Complete a full editorial pass on a provided manuscript excerpt (approximately 5,000 words). The project includes a developmental editorial letter (1,500-2,000 words), a copyedited manuscript with standard marks and queries, and a style sheet.
objectives: Apply developmental editing skills to identify structural and content issues
Write a professional editorial letter that is specific, constructive, and tactful
Execute a thorough copyedit with consistent style decisions
Demonstrate understanding of the full editorial process from manuscript to final proof
deliverables: Copyedited manuscript with standard marks and queries
500-word reflection on the editorial process and your decisions

criteria: Editorial letter identifies genuine structural and content issues
Feedback is specific, citing particular passages and explaining problems
Suggestions empower the author rather than prescribing solutions
Tone is professional, honest, and supportive
Grammar, spelling, and punctuation corrections are accurate
Author queries are appropriate and professionally phrased
Style sheet is complete and organized
Reflection demonstrates understanding of editorial judgment calls
Decisions are justified with reference to style guides
Overall process demonstrates professional editorial competence
Professional-quality editorial letter identifying genuine manuscript issues
Accurate and thorough copyedit with consistent style decisions
Complete style sheet and appropriate author queries
Thoughtful reflection on editorial process and judgment

editorial_project.description: Full editorial pass on a manuscript excerpt

publishing_analysis.description: Analysis of a publishing case study or industry trend

quizzes.description: Quizzes on publishing industry and editorial practice

lab_exercises.description: In-class copyediting, proofreading, and design exercises

final_portfolio.description: Professional editorial portfolio for the job market

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory, especially for editing workshops. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a letter grade deduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Editorial project components penalized 10% per day late.
course_policies.collaboration: Copyediting and proofreading exercises are individual. Discussion of editorial decisions is collaborative.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All editorial work must be your own. Style guide consultation is expected and encouraged.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with word processing software, access to Chicago Manual of Style Online, and LMS access.
